one_line_takeaway: By flipping a harmful prompt so it corrupts the left side that autoregressive LLMs read first, FlipAttack slips past guard models (~98% bypass) and jailbreaks 8 commercial LLMs at ~79% ASR in a single query.
key_finding: FlipAttack achieves ~78.97% average ASR across 8 LLMs, beating the runner-up black-box method ReNeLLM (56.64%) by 22.33 points, using only 1 query. It reaches 94.04% on GPT-4 Turbo, 88.08% on Claude 3.5 Sonnet, 86.73% on GPT-4, and 94.04% on Mixtral 8x22B. Against guard models it bypasses at 98.08% on average (100% on OpenAI Moderation and LLaMA Guard 2 8B). The mechanistic evidence: average perplexity is 74.75 for clean text X, 477.09 for right-side perturbation (X+N), and 815.93 for left-side perturbation (N+X), confirming left-side corruption is far more disruptive. Strong LLMs (GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) reverse the flip with >95% match rate, so the recovery task is not the bottleneck.
core_contribution: FlipAttack is a black-box jailbreak attack that breaks safety-aligned commercial LLMs in a single query. Its central idea is a 'left-side perturbation': because autoregressive LLMs are trained on next-token prediction, they tend to comprehend text left-to-right, so corrupting the left side of a prompt degrades understanding more than corrupting the right. The attack exploits this by flipping the harmful request (using only information already in the prompt, adding no external perturbation) so guard models and safety filters fail to recognize it, then supplies a 'flipping guidance' module that teaches the victim LLM to reverse the flip, understand, and execute the harmful intent. The paper's own contributions are: (1) the left-to-right understanding/perplexity insight; (2) an attack disguise module with 4 flipping modes (Flip Word Order, Flip Characters in Word, Flip Characters in Sentence, and a 'Fool Model Mode' that flips characters but tells the model to flip word order); (3) a flipping guidance module with 4 escalating variants (Vanilla, +CoT, +LangGPT role-playing, +Few-shot); and (4) two defense strategies (System Prompt Defense and Perplexity-based Guardrail Filter) which they show are ineffective against their own attack. GCG/AutoDAN/PAIR/ReNeLLM etc. are cited baselines from Related Work, NOT this paper's contributions.
threat_model: Adversary is a user with harmful intent (e.g., a hacker) who has ONLY black-box interface access to a commercial LLM (system prompt + user prompt), with no access to weights, gradients, or logits. Goal: transform a harmful request X into an attack prompt X' so that Fvictim(X') produces harmful content instead of a refusal, bypassing both the victim's alignment and any external guard model. The attack is designed to succeed within a single query (no iterative refinement), making it low-cost and stealthy.
limitations: (1) Effectiveness is uneven across models and harm categories: only 27.50% ASR on LLaMA 3.1 405B, and weak performance on 'physical harm', 'hate speech', and 'privacy violence' categories (best on malware/fraud/illegal activity). (2) Weak models (e.g., GPT-3.5 Turbo Vanilla only 30.58% ASR) often fail the flip-back task, so the attack depends on the victim being capable enough to decode. (3) CoT can backfire on GPT-4o mini (drops ASR to near 0) by making the model realize the task is harmful. (4) The few-shot variant leaks split fragments of the original harmful prompt (e.g., 'a bomb'), risking detection; authors flag better prompt-splitting as future work. (5) Stealthiness metric is perplexity on OPEN-source guard/LLMs only, since closed-model internals are inaccessible, so the stealth claim is an inferred proxy for commercial targets. (6) This is explicitly an offensive/red-team paper; the two proposed defenses (SPD, PGF) are shown ineffective, so no robust mitigation is delivered.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 harmful behaviors, Zou et al. 2023) as the main jailbreak benchmark, plus a 50-sample AdvBench subset for reproducible future comparison
100 benign prompts from Chao et al. 2024 (used for the left/right perplexity understanding-pattern study)
200 benign prompts from the Alpaca-safe dataset (He et al. 2024) for the flip-back match-rate experiment
7 GPT-4-assigned harm categories (malware, fraud, illegal activity, physical harm, hate speech, privacy violence, etc.) for category-level ASR breakdown
quantitative_results: Average ASR across 8 LLMs: FlipAttack 78.97% vs runner-up ReNeLLM 56.64% (+22.33 points)
Per-model ASR: GPT-4 Turbo 94.04%, GPT-4 86.73%, GPT-4o 90.77%, GPT-3.5 Turbo 88.65%, GPT-4o mini 61.92%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 88.08%, Mixtral 8x22B 94.04%, LLaMA 3.1 405B 27.50%
Average guard-model bypass rate 98.08%: OpenAI Moderation 100.00%, LLaMA Guard 2 8B 100.00%, WildGuard 7B 99.81%, LLaMA Guard 7B 98.65%, LLaMA Guard 3 8B 91.92%
Perplexity of understanding-pattern study (avg over 3 LLMs + 4 guard LLMs): clean X = 74.75, right-side X+N = 477.09, left-side N+X = 815.93
Stealthiness (avg PPL on guard/LLMs): FlipAttack 809.67 (highest) vs Origin 49.90, Caesar Cipher 258.10, ReNeLLM 15.56, ArtPrompt 3.23
Token count inflation: Origin 13.91 tokens avg vs Flip Characters in Word 30.67 and Flip Characters in Sentence 30.71 (disrupts tokenization)

Ablation highlights: Vanilla alone reaches 98.08% ASR on GPT-4 Turbo but only 30.58% on GPT-3.5 Turbo; adding CoT gives +16.92 pts on Claude 3.5 Sonnet; LangGPT lifts GPT-3.5 Turbo 39.04%->70.38%; few-shot adds +16.16 pts on GPT-3.5 Turbo
Cost: FlipAttack uses 1 query; ReNeLLM costs 5685 input tokens per attack; white-box methods need >24 GPU hours
Flip-back match rate >95% for GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet on benign prompts
baselines_compared: White-box: GCG (7.40% avg ASR), AutoDAN (37.04%), MAC (6.20%), COLD-Attack (5.60%)

Black-box: PAIR (20.79%), TAP (29.58%), Base64 (13.63%), GPTFuzzer (39.12%), DeepInception (23.30%), DRA (20.43%), ArtPrompt (5.44%), PromptAttack (2.16%), SelfCipher (5.22%), CodeChameleon (56.60%), ReNeLLM (56.64%, runner-up)
Guard models tested for bypass: OpenAI Moderation Endpoint, LLaMA Guard 7B, LLaMA Guard 2 8B, LLaMA Guard 3 8B, WildGuard 7B
